justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 as invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 as invariant:false). Each rule has detailed written justification clearly distinguishing state integrity constraints from configurable policy thresholds. Rules 2, 3, 5 explicitly use 'configurable'/'tunable' reasoning. Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both edge_cases and escalations (mid-dunning tier change interaction). Recommendation to store policies as configuration is embedded in testable assertions using named constants (RENEWAL_LEAD_DAYS, MAX_RENEWAL_RETRIES, PREMIUM_GRACE_DAYS).

observations.text: All 6 classifications correct with no errors

Justifications are exceptionally detailed — each explains *why* the classification holds using concrete domain reasoning (e.g., BR-1 explains billing ledger corruption, BR-4 explains audit trail impact)
Configurable thresholds expressed as named constants (RENEWAL_LEAD_DAYS, MAX_RENEWAL_RETRIES, PREMIUM_GRACE_DAYS) in testable assertions — implicitly recommending configuration over hard-coding
Grace period ambiguity surfaced with multiple resolution options and a recommendation
Additional edge cases beyond the rubric requirements — e.g., mid-dunning tier upgrade, trial expiry vs active distinction, voluntary vs involuntary cancellation
Confidence section honestly flags areas of uncertainty including the debatable nature of BR-4's invariant classification

The specific ambiguity called out in the rubric — 'Can a premium subscriber exhaust the grace period and get cancelled?' — is partially addressed (edge case mentions cancellation at 14-day boundary) but not explicitly stated as 'the spec implies the grace period replaces the 3-strike rule but doesn't say what happens after 14 days'
